title: System Boundaries

content.statement: What you include determines what you can control.
content.question: Is the problem you're solving scoped too narrowly to actually solve it?
content.equation: Performance = Outcomes Achieved divided by Scope Included, each rated 1-10, where Scope Included reflects how much of the relevant up/downstream picture you actually considered. The original 'Performance = f(scope)' is function notation, not a literal calculation.
sop: Write down the current scope of the problem you're trying to solve.
Rate how much of the relevant up/downstream system that scope actually includes, 1-10 (see calculation, page 5).
List one upstream and one downstream factor you've been excluding.
Redraw the boundary to include at least one of those factors.
Re-solve the problem at the new scope before declaring it fixed.
questions: Is the problem you're solving scoped too narrowly to actually solve it?
What's one factor just outside the boundary of your current fix that's actually driving the result?
If you zoomed out one level on your current problem, what would suddenly become relevant that you're ignoring?
content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10: Outcomes Achieved 4, Scope Included, how much of the full up/downstream picture you included, 3. Score = 4 divided by 3 = 1.3. A low Scope score paired with a low Outcomes score is your signal: the problem probably wasn't solved because it was framed too narrowly, not because the solution was wrong. Higher scope with higher outcomes means you're solving the actual system, not a fragment of it.
content.closing_statement: How you draw the boundary decides what you can fix.

caption:
Principle Nº 124: System Boundaries

What you include determines what you can control.

Ask yourself: Is the problem you're solving scoped too narrowly to actually solve it?
